text: This video demonstrates a laparoscopic assisted right colectomy and cholecystectomy for a neuroendocrine tumor by Dr. Bhiman Khalil.

Neuroendocrine tumors are rare, occurring in one to two million people. They are comprised of embryonic cells related to both endocrine producing cells and nerve cells. They can either be benign or malignant. They can be located anywhere in the body, but most commonly occur in the lungs, the brain and the GI tract.

Pathologists recognize five commonest types, pheochromocytoma, Merker cell, carcinoid, thymoma, and thyroid carcinoma. Clinical symptoms are usually not present, but specific symptoms are sometimes produced by carcinoids by secreting an as yet unidentified hormone producing most commonly a distinctive flushing syndrome, diarrhea, heart valvular lesions, or cramping. Insulinomas by secreting insulin producing low blood glucose levels, recurrent headache, lethargy, impaired vision, seizures, comma, and permanent neurological damage. Gastrinomas, by secreting gastrin, producing severe ulcer disease, typically part of the Zollinger-Ellison syndrome with abdominal pains and diarrhea. Glucogonomas by producing decreased blood levels of amino acids, anemia, diarrhea, and weight loss. Pheochromocytomas by secreting epinephrine. This can be associated with skin sensations, flank pain, elevated heart rate, elevated blood pressure, palpitations, excessive sweating or headaches.

VIP omas, producing vasoactive intestinal peptide, that is the VIP, can produce watery diarrhea, low blood potassium, and loss of gastric acid. Somatostatin omas, by producing somatostatin, producing a clinical pentad of diabetes mellitus, cholelithiasis, weight loss, steatorrhea, and diarrhea. sometimes with hypochlorhydria and achlorhydria.

Diagnoses of neuroendocrine tumors can be made in several ways. Imaging with CAT scans, MRIs, and radioactive octreotide scan. Endoscopy using colonoscopy, gastroscopy, bronchoscopy, and endoscopic ultrasound. And finally, hormone assays.

Treatment consists of a combination of surgery, chemotherapy, radionucleotide therapy, and or radiation therapy. This patient presented with blood in the stool and underwent a colonoscopy which showed a tumor of the transverse colon. Pathology showed it to be a malignant neuroendocrine tumor.
Serial CAT scan cuts show a normal liver, and then the colon primary.

A yellow circle highlights the area of the colon lesion. The laparoscopic dissection starts with the lysis of adhesions from a previous surgery. The surgeon proceeds to dissect across the gastrocolic ligament, around the hepatic flexure, down the right gutter, and finally around the cecum and appendix. Adhesions from the small bowel to the abdominal wall are being carefully taken down using a hook cautery to access the transverse colon.

The surgeon begins the initial dissection to separate the colon from the stomach, lifting the omentum to reveal the colon. The endoscopic tattoo marking the tumor site is visible, and the tumor is palpated with an instrument.

The surgeon opens the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the lesser space behind the stomach. The dissection extends to connect with the previous hepatic flexure dissection. Lifting the omentum exposes the tumor growing through the serosa. Elevating the transverse colon provides a clear view of the hepatic flexure and cecum. Dissection continues around the base of the cecum and appendix, preparing the colon for delivery through a small abdominal incision for resection and anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an incidental cholecystectomy for symptomatic gallstones. The gallbladder is grasped and retracted to expose the base, allowing visualization of the cystic artery and cystic duct, which are then located, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon opens the peritoneal covering at the base and begins dissection superiorly to expose the cystic artery area. The cystic duct is progressively encircled during the procedure.

A small tear in the gallbladder wall is clipped to prevent bile and stone leakage. The cystic duct is encircled, clipped twice on the common duct side, and divided. Dissection continues along the undersurface to encircle the cystic artery, which is then clearly identified, clipped, and divided.
The surgeon frees the gallbladder from its hepatic attachments, amputates it from the liver surface, and secures it with a clamp for removal alongside the colon.

A small upper abdominal incision is made to remove the gallbladder and right colon. The gross specimen is displayed, showing the gallbladder and right colon. A closer view reveals serosal invasion by the tumor, and upon opening the specimen, the primary tumor is visible.
